coredata.dc:title: Dual signal amplification platform based on recombinant polymerase amplification and fluorescence-enhanced nanoparticles for sensitive detection of Salmonella
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Fengrong
Feng, Fan
Yuan, Yun
Kong, Ruixue
Fu, Qiang
Ji, Dandan
Liu, Haiyun
coredata.dc:description: Salmonella is a common foodborne pathogen, and its detection is essential for managing public food safety. Lateral flow assay (LFA) strips are well suited for the purpose as they are inexpensive and easy to use. However, the sensitive detection of low-concentration targets requires the use of signal amplification strategies. This paper presents a LFA for the ultrasensitive detection of Salmonella based on recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) and fluorescence-enhanced composite nanospheres dSiO2@CD@SiO2 (dCDS). RPA is capable of rapid (∼20 min) exponential amplification of bacterial DNA and does not require complex instrumentation. Mesoporous silica (dSiO2) has a large specific surface area and radial pore size, making it an excellent structural material for internal assembly. Nanoparticles obtained by loading carbon dots (CDs) had a strong fluorescence signal and stability. We used dCDSs as reporter molecules, which captured a large number of DNA-dCDS complexes on the test line of the LFA. With double signal amplification, the RPA and dCDS-based LFA showed greatly improved sensitivity: The limits of detection for Salmonella in buffer and milk were 3.8 CFU/mL and 36 CFU/mL, respectively. This study provides an inexpensive strategy for the sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lateral flow assay
Recombinase polymerase amplification
Mesoporous silica
Carbon dot
Bacteria detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the Major projects of tackling key industrial of Shandong’s New-traditional Kinetic Energy Conversion, Shandong Province Key R&D Program (Major Science and technology innovation project; 2021CXGC010802) and green manufacturing system integration projects program of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) of China ([2017]373).

This work was supported by grants from the Major projects of tackling key industrial of Shandong's New-traditional Kinetic Energy Conversion, Shandong Province Key R&D Program (Major Science and technology innovation project; 2021CXGC010802) and green manufacturing system integration projects program of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) of China ([2017]373).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Construction of RPA and dCDS-based LFA for Salmonella detection. (A) Schematic diagram of composite nanoparticle preparation. (B) Salmonella DNA extraction and amplification. (C) Schematic diagram of LFA detection of Salmonella.
(A, C, E) TEM images of SiO2, CDs, and CDSs. (B, D, F) SEM images of SiO2, CDs, and CDSs.

(A) Zeta potential of dSiO2, NH2– dSiO2, dCD, COOH-dCDS, and SA-dCDS. (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra of dSiO2, CDs, dCD, and SA-dCDS. (C) Photographs of test strips detecting different concentrations of Salmonella; concentrations from left to right are 0, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107 CFU/mL. Non-linear fitting curves of bacterial concentration and grayscale values of T-line. (D) Linear fit curves of some bacterial concentrations in buffer solution and grayscale values of T-line.

(A) Photos of test strips detecting six different bacteria, including Escherichia coli, Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus aureus, Shigella flexneri, Vibrio parahaemolyticus, and Salmonella typhimurium, and grayscale values of the T-line. (B, C) Average grayscale value of the T-line for 10 test strips detecting Salmonella at 102 (B) and 104 CFU/mL (C), respectively.

(A) Photographs of test strips detecting different concentrations (from left to right: 0, 101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107 CFU/mL) of Salmonella in milk. Non-linear fitting curves of bacterial concentration in milk and grayscale value of T-line. (B) Linear fit curves of different bacterial concentrations (101, 102, 103, 104, 105) in milk and grayscale values of T-line.
Comparison of the sensitivity of different LFA for pathogens detection.
Recoveries of LFA for Salmonella detection in milk (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Dual signal amplification platform based on recombinant polymerase amplification and fluorescence-enhanced nanoparticles for sensitive detection of Salmonella

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Science)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salmonella is a common foodborne pathogen, and its detection is essential for managing public food safety. Lateral flow assay (LFA) strips are well suited for the purpose as they are inexpensive and easy to use. However, the sensitive detection of low-concentration targets requires the use of signal amplification strategies. This paper presents a LFA for the ultrasensitive detection of Salmonella based on recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) and fluorescence-enhanced composite nanospheres dSiO2@CD@SiO2 (dCDS). RPA is capable of rapid (∼20 min) exponential amplification of bacterial DNA and does not require complex instrumentation. Mesoporous silica (dSiO2) has a large specific surface area and radial pore size, making it an excellent structural material for internal assembly. Nanoparticles obtained by loading carbon dots (CDs) had a strong fluorescence signal and stability. We used dCDSs as reporter molecules, which captured a large number of DNA-dCDS complexes on the test line of the LFA. With double signal amplification, the RPA and dCDS-based LFA showed greatly improved sensitivity: The limits of detection for Salmonella in buffer and milk were 3.8 CFU/mL and 36 CFU/mL, respectively. This study provides an inexpensive strategy for the sensitive detection of pathogenic bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food-borne pathogens have become the most serious issue in food safety and the related diseases pose a major threat to global human health [1,2]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), 1 in 10 individuals are infected with a food-borne pathogen each year [3]. Salmonella is a major foodborne pathogen that can contaminate red meat, fish, poultry, milk, and egg products. Consequently, strict international standards have been established to limit Salmonella content in different foods [4,5]. Therefore, the sensitive detection of Salmonella is important for the effective management of related foodborne diseases.

Although traditional culture-based analysis is the gold standard for identifying pathogenic microorganisms, they usually require long culture and identification processes, which are time-consuming, laborious, and lack sensitivity [6,7]. Among alternative assays, polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-based methods require expensive specialized equipment, skilled technicians, and long processing procedures [8,9], while enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays have limited reproducibility, are prone to false positives, and are strongly influenced by temperature [10].

In recent years, the lateral flow assay (LFA) has gained popularity as a point-of-care testing technique for various pathogens or compounds, such as bacteria, viruses, mycotoxins, and pollutants [11–14]. Recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) is used as a signal enhancement method to increase LFA sensitivity [15,16]. As an alternative to conventional PCR, RPA can be completed in approximately 20 min with exponential growth of amplified products due to isothermal operation at a constant temperature [17]. RPA-LFAs are easily assessed by measuring the accumulation of reporting probes. The most widely utilized reporter molecules in the RPA-LFA system are gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) [18,19]. However, as a traditional colorimetric label, AuNPs show relatively low sensitivity and quantifiability [20,21]. Compared to colorimetric labelling, fluorescent particles are more sensitive because they have better contrast and lower background signals [22–24]. Compared to conventional metal quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs)—a type of quantum dot—are chemically inert, photostable, less cytotoxic, and environmentally friendly [25]. Unfortunately, the detection of low concentration targets is limited in LFAs using individual CDs [26,27]. For further signal enhancement, signal molecules could be loaded on a carrier to increase its density, thereby increasing the sensitivity of the LFA [28–31].

In this work, we developed an LFA based on RPA and a fluorescent-enhanced nanoparticle dSiO2@CD@SiO2 (prepared by loading CDs with mesoporous silica; dSiO2dCDS) to detect Salmonella. In this system, Salmonella DNA is first amplified through RPA (first signal amplification), bound to the fluorescent probe dCDS through the interaction of biotin and streptavidin (second signal amplification), and the DNA-dCDS complex is captured on the T-line of the test strip. The unique mesoporous structure of dSiO2 makes it an ideal carrier material for enrichment and signal amplification, and allows for the integration of a large number of signal molecules; therefore, composite nanoparticles prepared by loading CDs into mesoporous silica can greatly enhance their fluorescence performance. Compared with traditional colorimetric RPA-LFA, the dual signal amplification system improved the sensitivity by more than 30-fold. Furthermore, quantitative detection of Salmonella can be achieved rapidly and conveniently through analysis of gray values. The method is isothermal, reducing processing time and eliminating the need for specialized equipment. Overall, this study provides a highly sensitive detection method for rapid detection of Salmonella, particularly in resource-limited sites.

RPA basic amplification kits were obtained from TwistDx Ltd. (Cambridge, UK). TIANamp Bacteria DNA Kits were purchased from Tiangen Biotech Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Mouse anti-digoxin (anti-DIG) antibody and rabbit anti-streptavidin (anti-SA) antibody were purchased from Abcam (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), Tween-20, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 0.01 M) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES), n-octyltrimethoxysilane (OTMS), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide sodium salt (sulfo-NHS) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Triethanolamine (TEA), sodium salicylate (NaSal), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ammonia solution, citric acid monohydrate, urea, hydrochloric acid, methanol, ethanol, and toluene were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Nitrocellulose (NC) membrane, conjugate pad, sample pad and absorbent pad were obtained from JieYi BioTech Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). PVC backplane was obtained from Jinbiao Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All primers were synthesized by Accurate Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hunan, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from Millipore Milli-Q system. Milk was purchased from local supermarket.

dSiO2 was prepared as previously described, with minor adjustments [32,33]. First, 50 mL water and 136 mg TEA were mixed and stirred at 80 °C for 30 min. Then, 760 mg CTAB and 400 mg sodium salicylate were added and left for 1 h. After adding 8 mL TEOS, the mixture was gently stirred at 80 °C for another 2 h. The dSiO2 molecules were separated by centrifugation and re-dispersed in a solution of 6 mL hydrochloric acid and 100 mL methanol, which was then agitated at 60 °C for 12 h to remove excess surfactant. This last step was repeated once. The dSiO2 was dried overnight in a freeze dryer and then ground and stored.

We transferred 100 mg of dried dSiO2 into a three-necked flask, added 15 mL of anhydrous toluene and 3 mL of APTES, and stirred the mixture at 80 °C for 6 h. After the reaction is completed, the product was washed with ethanol to eliminate excess APTES and then dried and ground to obtain amino-modified dSiO2.

To load dSiO2 with dCDs, we dissolved 500 mg of citric acid monohydrate and 1 g of urea in 10 mL of deionized water, added 100 mg of dry dSiO2, incubated the mixture for 2 h and transferred it to a hydrothermal reactor set to 180 °C for 5 h, cooled the mixture to room temperature, centrifuged the product, and washed away any free quantum dots.

To generate silica shells, 12.5 µL TEOS was injected every hour while 6 mg of dCD was re-dispersed in a solution of 10 mL ethanol, 2.5 mL water, and 625 µL ammonia [34]. After 10 h, dCDs with a silica shell (dCDSs) were collected after centrifugation and ethanol washes.

We re-dispersed 40 mg dCDS nanospheres in 80 mL ethanol, 2 mL ammonia, and 800 µL APTES solution, which was then agitated at room temperature for 8 h. After being cleaned with ethanol, the amino-dCDS spheres were resuspended in 40 mL DMF containing succinic anhydride (5 mg/mL) and stirred for 4 h.

The obtained carboxyl nanospheres (10 mg) were washed, dispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH = 6.0) to a concentration of 1 mg/mL, and mixed with 20 mg EDC and 10 mg NHS under shaking for 30 min. After centrifugation and removal of the supernatant, the microspheres were redispersed in 10 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) and incubated with streptavidin (20 μL) with shaking for 2 h, followed by surface closure with BSA (20 %, 10 μL) for 2 h. Finally, they were redistributed in PBS (pH = 7.4) with 0.2 % BSA and preserved at 4 °C.

All strains were cultured overnight at 37℃ and then serially diluted 10-fold in physiological saline. The bacterial concentration was calculated by standard plate counting method. Following the manufacturer's protocol (TIANGEN BIOTECH Ltd.), 1 mL of bacterial culture was collected by high-speed centrifugation to collect bacterial cells. The cells were resuspended in 200 μL of GA buffer and mixed with 20 μL of proteinase K. After mixing, 220 μL of GB buffer was added, and the mixture was incubated for 10 min. Then, 200 μL of ethanol was added to the mixture, which was then transferred to a adsorption column. The column was centrifuged at high speed, and the waste liquid was discarded. Next, 500 μL of GD buffer was added to the column, centrifuged, and the waste liquid was discarded. This process was repeated once more with 600 μL of wash buffer. Finally, the adsorption column was placed in a clean centrifuge tube, and 100 μL of TE elution buffer was added to the column. The eluted solution collected after centrifugation is the DNA template.

According to the S. Enteritidis target gene gyrA sequence, five sets of primers were designed using Primer 6.0 software. The forward primers were biotin-labeled and the reverse primers were digoxin-modified. The specific primer sequences are listed in Table S1.

According to the manufacturer’s protocol (TwistDx Ltd.), each reaction tube contained 29.5 μL rehydration buffer, 2.4 μL pre-primer and 2.4 μL post-primer, 2.0 μL template DNA and 11.2 μL water. After rapid centrifugation, all contents were transferred to a reaction tube containing lyophilized enzyme particles, to which 2.5 μL of 280 mM magnesium acetate was added. The mixture was shaken and briefly centrifuged before incubation at 37 °C for 20 min. RPA products were detected using agarose gel electrophoresis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the lateral flow strip
ce:section.ce:para: LFA is made of five parts: a sample pad, conjugate pad, NC membrane, absorbent pad, and PVC backing. The sample pads were pre-treated with PBS containing 0.1 % Tween-20 and 1 % BSA before being left overnight in a 37 ℃ thermostatic oven. The conjugate pads were pre-treated with a PBS solution containing 3 % BSA, 5 % sucrose, 1 % Tween-20, 1 % NaCl, and SA-dCDS and dried at 37 °C.

The NC membrane was coated with anti-DIG antibody (1 mg/mL) and anti-SA antibody (1 mg/mL) to create the T-line and control line (C-line), respectively. The treated NC membranes were dried overnight at 37 °C and then cut to 4.0 mm width. To guarantee adequate solution migration, each part was assembled sequentially on the PVC backing with a 3 mm overlap (Fig. 1 ). The completed test strips were kept in a dry dark area for storage.

DNA from different concentrations of Salmonella was extracted and used as a template for RPA. After amplification, 10 μL of product was pipetted into 50 μL PBS before applying it dropwise to the sample pad for detection. The samples were assayed three times for each concentration under the same conditions over 10 min each. The qualitative results were obtained by visual observation of the bands under UV light. For quantitative analysis, a smartphone (Redmi K30 Pro) was used to collect images of the bands, which were uploaded to analyse the grayscale value and fit the bacterial concentration to the average grayscale value of the T-line region. All tests were performed three times under the same conditions.

To validate the specificity of LFA, other common foodborne pathogens bacteria (Table S2) were tested using the same method, and the reproducibility of LFA was verified by detecting 102 and 104 CFU/mL of Salmonella 10 times each.

Standard solutions of Salmonella with known concentrations were spiked into milk samples, and the proposed method was used for analysis. The real samples were exposed to air and left at room temperature for different periods (0 d, 1 d, 2 d), and Salmonella contamination in these samples was detected using both LFA and standard culture-based methods (ISO 6579).

The preparation of SA-dCDS is shown in Fig. 1A. Firstly, dSiO2 templates were prepared using the anion-assisted method with cetyltrimethylammonium bromide and sodium salicylate as the structural guide. The silica carriers had a dendritic central radial pore channel, mean size of 200 nm (Fig. 2 A), and dense irregular distribution of pores on their outer surface (Fig. 2B). According to the nitrogen isothermal adsorption and desorption curves (Fig. S1A), dSiO2 has a typical mesoporous structure that, based on the large specific surface area (713.48 m2/g) and pore volume (1.81 cm3/g), has a strong load-bearing capacity. dSiO2 had an average pore size of 13.3 nm (Fig. S1B), which provides sufficient space for the loading of CDs.

After the modification of amino groups on the surface of dSiO2, urea and citric acid were used as precursors to generate CDs under high temperature and pressure; the carboxyl groups of the CDs underwent a dehydration condensation reaction with the amino of dSiO2, which fixed the CDs to the pore channels of the dSiO2. The obtained dCDs were bright blue under UV light. However, the CDs could not always be clearly observed because of their size or location in the dSiO2 channels (Fig. 2C, D). Finally, the dCD was encapsulated with a silica shell, which filled the pores and formed a dCDS structure with a rough spherical appearance (Fig. 2E, F). After grafting with the carboxyl surface, streptavidin was covalently conjugated to the spheres to produce SA-dCDS.

The successful synthesis of dCDS was determined by measuring the zeta potential (Fig. 3 A). The zeta potential of dSiO2 was –31.8 mV owing to the presence of negatively charged silanol groups on the exterior, and the surface potential changed to 27.2 mV after successful modification of the positively charged amino group. After carboxyl grafting, the surface of dCDS became negative (-35.5 mV), and the potential changed slightly after the dehydration condensation of the carboxyl group with the amino group on the surface of the SA-dCDS. The maximum UV absorption peak of the SA-dCDS at 220 nm (Fig. 3B) also proved the successful coupling of SA.

The nanoparticles were characterized by UV and fluorescence spectroscopy at different stages of synthesis to evaluate their optical performance. Both CDs and dCDs had a maximum UV absorption peak of 340 nm (Fig. 3B), and the best emission wavelength (450 nm, Fig. S2A). This indicates that the CDs were successfully loaded onto the dSiO2; the absorption characteristics of CD did not change significantly between stages. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of the dCDSs was slightly reduced after encapsulation with the silica shells (Fig. S2B). Because of the silica shell, the internal CDs are largely protected from the external environment and the dCDS has good stability (Fig. S3A). The dCDS also showed good thermal stability (Fig. S3B).

In this system, RPA has a significant impact on the sensitivity of LFA, so we conducted several experiments to determine the optimal reaction conditions for RPA amplification. Five different groups of primers were devised based on the DNA of Salmonella (Table S1). All except the second set of primers produced unwanted bands (Fig. S4A), thus the second set was chosen for the RPA reaction. RPA was performed at 5 gradient temperatures to investigate the optimal amplification temperature. Band colour was darkest at 37 °C (Fig. S4B), therefore 37 °C was selected as the optimum temperature for the RPA experiment. Finally, we performed RPA reactions at five different time lengths and found that the band colours converged between 20 and 25 min (Fig. S4C), which indicated that amplification was almost complete at 20 min; thus, the subsequent RPA experiments were conducted over 20 min.

To ensure the sufficient release and migration of dCDS on the conjugate pad, LFA detection was performed using buffer compositions with different ratios of components. When using PBS or PBS containing 3 % BSA and 1 % NaCl, the release of dCDS was not ideal (Fig. S5A, B). The addition of 2.5 % sucrose and 0.5 % Tween-20 improved the release of dCDS, but some dCDS still failed to migrate (Fig. S5C, D). After doubling the concentrations of sucrose and Tween-20, dCDS on the conjugate pad was released effectively, and clear T and C lines were observed (Fig. S5E). Therefore, for subsequent experiments, the buffer composition was selected as 3 % BSA, 5 % sucrose, 1 % Tween-20, and 1 % NaCl.

Fig. 1 illustrates the setup of an ultra-sensitive LFA biosensor based on RPA and streptavidin-conjugated dCDS (SA-dCDS) for the detection of Salmonella. The dCDS-based LFA consists of a sample pad, conjugate pad, NC membrane with a T-line and C-line, and absorbent pad that provides capillary force (Fig. 1C). Anti-DIG and anti-SA antibodies are distributed on the T- and C-line, respectively. After the Salmonella DNA is amplified (Fig. 1B), the amplification product is dropped onto the sample pad, where it moves by capillary force in the direction of the absorbent pad. During this process, the SA-dCDS label on the conjugate pad is rapidly dissolved and bound to the target DNA amplification product through the biotin-avidin reaction. The formed DNA-dCDS compound keeps moving along the NC membrane and is immobilized by the anti-DIG antibody on the T-line. The rest of the SA-dCDSs are immobilized by the anti-SA antibody on the C-line. In the case of a positive sample, the T-line shows fluorescent bands under UV light; in the case of a negative sample, only the C-line shows a fluorescent band. It is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of the T-line is positively correlated with the concentration of Salmonella, which allows for quantitative analysis.

The sensitivity of the LFA was explored using a range of Salmonella concentrations. As the concentration of bacteria decreased, the T-line became thinner and darker (i.e. less DNA-dCDS was captured). With no bacteria in the sample, background signal was generated on the T-line (barely observable with the naked eye) as a result of the nonspecific binding of the captured antibody to DNA-dCDS (Fig. 3C). The visual limit of detection (LOD) of the test strips was 10 CFU/mL. For the quantitative analysis of the LFA, we analysed the average grayscale value of the T-line at different bacterial concentrations using ImageJ software and found that the grayscale value increased with increasing bacterial concentration. We fitted the T-line grayscale values nonlinearly to the bacterial concentration (Fig. 3C, R2 = 0.993) and found that the antibody binding sites on the T-line were saturated at bacterial concentrations > 105 CFU/mL, beyond which the grayscale value no longer increased significantly. At a bacterial concentration of less than 105 CFU/mL, the grayscale value was linearly related to the bacterial concentration (Fig. 3D, R2 = 0.984), LOD was 3.8 CFU/mL. Compared to several RPA-LFA tests (Table 1 ), the utilization of dCDS resulted in a more than 30-fold reduction in the LOD, whereas RPA-LFA tests exhibited a more than 700-fold reduction in LOD compared to traditional LFA [39]. Therefore, within the proposed method, RPA played a more significant role in the substantial decrease of LOD. However, it is undeniable that fluorescence-enhanced nanoparticles are also indispensable in the dual signal amplification system. Furthermore, in comparison with other methods of pathogen detection, this study still retains an advantage in terms of detection sensitivity and efficiency (Table S3).

To verify the specificity of the LFA, five other common foodborne pathogenic bacteria were selected as interfering terms, including Escherichia coli, Listeria monocytogenes, Staphylococcus aureus, Shigella flexneri, and Vibrio parahaemolyticus (the details for all interfering bacteria are shown in Table S2). Only the test strip for Salmonella detection had a bright T-line, while the other five test strips had only a weak fluorescent background signal (caused by non-specific binding) on the T-line, which was negligible and almost consistent with the negative sample detection, implying that the dCDS-based LFA has good specificity (Fig. 4 A).

To investigate the reproducibility of the LFA, ten test strips were used to detect Salmonella at 102 and 104 CFU/mL, respectively, and their grayscale values were calculated (Fig. 4B, C). The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 3.46 % (102 CFU/mL) and 4.27 % (104 CFU/mL), indicating that our test strips have good reproducibility.

In this study, we validated a LFA using RPA and dCDS fluorescent nanoparticles for ultrasensitive detection of Salmonella. RPA enabled rapid amplification of bacterial DNA under isothermal conditions; the mesoporous silica provided a large pore size and specific surface area for high-density loading and uniform assembly of CDs; and the accumulation of CDs enhanced fluorescence intensity. With dual signal amplification, the LFA has high sensitivity with a visual LOD of 10 CFU/mL for Salmonella in buffer and a LOD of 3.8 CFU/mL obtained using grayscale analysis, the use of dCDS resulted in an increase in sensitivity of at least 30-fold. In addition, quantitative detection can be easily achieved through analysis of gray values. The successful detection of Salmonella in milk demonstrates the potential of our technique for real-world application. In addition to its excellent sensitivity, the RPA and dCDS-based LFA has a short detection time, good specificity, and excellent reproducibility. Moreover, by simply changing the primers for RPA amplification, LFA can be applied to detect any bacteria, which expands its potential for future applications considerably. However, a series of detections on spiked milk samples showed that the decrease in LOD indicated an inevitable influence of food matrix on the proposed method, which needs to be addressed in future studies.

Fengrong Cao: Data curation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Fan Feng: Supervision. Yun Yuan: Visualization. Ruixue Kong: Visualization. Qiang Fu: . Dandan Ji: Funding acquisition, Resources. Haiyun Liu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Major projects of tackling key industrial of Shandong’s New-traditional Kinetic Energy Conversion, Shandong Province Key R&D Program (Major Science and technology innovation project; 2021CXGC010802) and green manufacturing system integration projects program of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) of China ([2017]373).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109823.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Food-related illness and death in the United States

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.S. Mead, L. Slutsker, V. Dietz, L.F. McCaig, J.S. Bresee, C. Shapiro, P.M. Griffin, R.V. Tauxe, Food-related illness and death in the United States, Emerging Infectious Diseases, 5 (1999) 607-625. https://doi.org/10.3201/eid0505.990502.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Foodborne illness acquired in the united states-major pathogens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Scallan, R.M. Hoekstra, F.J. Angulo, R.V. Tauxe, M.A. Widdowson, S.L. Roy, J.L. Jones, P.M. Griffin, Foodborne illness acquired in the United States-Major pathogens, Emerging Infectious Diseases, 17 (2011) 7-15. https://doi.org/10.3201/eid1701.P11101.
